title: Face Frame Base Kitchen Cabinet Carcass, 24"
summary: A 24"-wide base cabinet carcass built from a single ripped plywood strip per side, with a scrap-plywood top support and a face frame sized to give a run of cabinets room to align on an imperfect wall.
description:
Each side panel is cut as one full-height piece with the toe kick notched directly out of it, rather than built from a separate box plus a separate toe-kick assembly — a toe-kick pattern block, used to trace the same notch onto every side panel in a run, is what keeps that cut identical across multiple cabinets built from the same plan.

The face frame is cut 1/2" wider than the carcass underneath it on purpose — the source plan's own reasoning is that this gives real wiggle room when a whole run of cabinets goes in against a wall that isn't perfectly flat or square, letting the face frames line up perfectly even when the carcasses behind them can't quite match. The scrap plywood strips left over from ripping the sides and shelves aren't waste — they become the top support that squares up the back of the cabinet and gives the countertop plywood something solid to attach to later.

This file works out one specific 24"-wide cabinet as a concrete example — the source plan is a general carcass-building method meant to scale to whatever cabinet width a kitchen run actually needs, so treat these dimensions as a worked example, not a fixed size.

tools.note: Notches the toe kick out of each side panel.

materials.name: Cabinet-grade plywood, 3/4", ripped to 22-1/2" strips, 4x8 sheet
Pine, 1x4, 8 ft, or plywood scrap, for supports
Plywood, 1/4", for backs and end panels
materials.note: Also used to finish exposed cabinet ends and the toe kick after installation.

cutList.note: Notch out the toe kick with a jigsaw before assembly.
Carcass width is 1/2" less than the 24" finished cabinet width.
Also used to hang the cabinet on the wall and to square the back.
Face frame is 24" wide overall — 1/2" wider than the carcass, giving alignment room across a run of cabinets.

steps.title: Rip the plywood and cut the sides
steps.body: Rip a full sheet of plywood into 22-1/2"-wide strips, saving the leftover scrap for the top supports or corner bracing. Cross-cut two 34-1/2" side panels from the strips and notch out the toe kick with a jigsaw. If you want pocket-hole face frame attachment, drill 3/4" pocket holes on the inside front edges of the sides now.
materials: Cabinet-grade plywood, 3/4", ripped to 22-1/2" strips, 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Cut and attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Cut the bottom shelf from the ripped strips, remembering the carcass runs 1/2" narrower than the desired finished cabinet width. Drill 3/4" pocket holes on its 22-1/2"-long sides and attach to the side panels with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Also drill 3/4" pocket holes facing forward on the underside of the bottom shelf for attaching the face frame later.
materials: Cabinet-grade plywood, 3/4", ripped to 22-1/2" strips, 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Add the front top support
steps.body: Use a scrap plywood strip for the front top support. This squares up the cabinet and gives you something to attach countertop plywood to later — it gets covered in a later step, so it doesn't need to look pretty. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and attach with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws; also drill pocket holes facing forward for the face frame if using that attachment method.
materials: Pine, 1x4, 8 ft, or plywood scrap, for supports

steps.title: Add the back top support
steps.body: Add the back top support the same way, again using scrap plywood if you have it. This board is what you'll screw into a wall stud to hang the cabinet, and it also squares up the back. You can attach the back panel now or wait until after drawers or shelves are installed, whichever is easier.
materials: Pine, 1x4, 8 ft, or plywood scrap, for supports

steps.title: Build the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame from the stiles and rails with pocket holes and glue. If the wood is splitting, set your jig for 1/2" stock and use 1" pocket hole screws instead. The face frame runs 1/2" wider than the carcass — this gives you room to line up face frames perfectly across a run of cabinets, even on an imperfect wall.

steps.title: Attach the face frame and finish exposed ends
steps.body: Attach the finished face frame to the front of the carcass. For any cabinet end that will be exposed, finish it with 1/4" plywood matched to your face frame species — useful if you're using a lower-cost sheet good for the carcass and a nicer hardwood for the visible face frame and doors.
materials: Plywood, 1/4", for backs and end panels

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every visible surface. Leave a 1/2" reveal gap between doors and drawers on the same cabinet, and 1/4" per cabinet between neighboring cabinets, when it comes time to hang doors.
